title: Flaky test: TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/cdi (CDI adjustment expected to fail but got nil)
author: GordonTheTurtle
body:
## Description

`TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/cdi` intermittently reports `assertion failed: expected an error, got nil` when an NRI plugin attempts to apply a CDI (Container Device Interface) device adjustment during container creation. The test asserts that the CDI adjustment is rejected with an error, but occasionally the daemon returns nil.

## First observed

2026-07-13, run [29264907344](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29264907344/job/86868071542) (`bump_bolt` PR branch, `test (amd64, graphdriver) / integration (amd64, ubuntu-24.04)` job).

## Failure output

```
nri_test.go:135: assertion failed: expected an error, got nil
--- FAIL: TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/cdi (0.03s)
    --- PASS: TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/hooks (0.01s)
    --- FAIL: TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/cdi (0.03s)
    --- PASS: TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/cpu (0.01s)
```

The NRI plugin log shows the plugin lost and re-established its connection just before the CDI adjustment was attempted:
```
Connection to the runtime lost.
Registering plugin 00-nri-test-plugin...
Configuring plugin for runtime docker/dev (NRI version v0.12.0)...
```

## Cross-reference

The same test passed in:
- master run [29287465296](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29287465296/job/86943802318) amd64/graphdriver — PASS TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/cdi (0.00s)
- master run [29287465296](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29287465296/job/86943800722) amd64/snapshotter — PASS TestNRIContainerCreateUnsupportedAdj/cdi (0.03s)

The failing PR (bump_bolt) only bumped the bbolt dependency, which is completely unrelated to NRI/CDI functionality.

## Root cause hypothesis

Race condition in the NRI plugin lifecycle: the test registers a plugin, requests an unsupported CDI device adjustment, and expects an error. When the plugin's connection is re-established (after "Connection to the runtime lost"), the CDI rejection path may not yet be active, causing the daemon to silently accept the adjustment and return nil instead of an error.

## Related
- #37306 — flaky tests epic

_Flagged by automated CI flakiness scan — 2026-07-14._

title: Flaky test: DockerSwarmSuite/TestAPISwarmServiceConstraintRole (swarm service scheduling timeout on arm64)
author: GordonTheTurtle
body:
## Description

`TestDockerSwarmSuite/TestAPISwarmServiceConstraintRole` intermittently times out on arm64 CI runners. The test creates a three-node swarm, deploys a service with a role-based placement constraint, and waits for it to become healthy. On arm64 the swarm initialization or task scheduling appears to take longer than the test's timeout allows.

## First observed

2026-07-13, run [29287465296](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29287465296/job/86943869048) (`master`, `test (arm64, snapshotter) / integration-cli (DockerSwarmSuite)` job).

## Failure output

```
docker_api_swarm_service_test.go:358: timeout hit after 30s: first check never completed
service.go:56: assertion failed: error is not nil: failed to connect to the docker API at
    unix:///tmp/docker-integration/d311f3b0c3bd5.sock; check if the path is correct and if
    the daemon is running: dial unix ...: connect: no such file or directory
--- FAIL: TestDockerSwarmSuite/TestAPISwarmServiceConstraintRole (43.76s)
```

## Cross-reference

The **same test passed** on amd64/graphdriver in the identical run:
`test (amd64, graphdriver) / integration-cli (DockerSwarmSuite)` → `PASS TestDockerSwarmSuite/TestAPISwarmServiceConstraintRole (14.17s)`

The amd64 run took ~14s; the arm64 run failed after 43s with a socket-not-found error, suggesting the Docker daemon crashed or was too slow to start on the arm64 runner under swarm load.

## Root cause hypothesis

Timing/resource sensitivity on arm64 GitHub Actions runners: swarm raft leader election + service scheduling races against a 30s timeout that is tight on arm64 hardware. Related to the general class of swarm timing flakes.

## Related
- #34051 — Flaky test: DockerSwarmSuite.TestSwarmLockUnlockCluster (same suite, similar timing race)
- #23516 — Flaky test: TestAPISwarmNodeDrainPause  
- #37306 — flaky tests epic

_Flagged by automated CI flakiness scan — 2026-07-14._

title: Windows: containerd image store can't use the CimFS snapshotter — dockerd doesn't configure the differ order and offers no passthrough
author: inistor
body:
### Summary
On Windows, Docker's containerd image store can *select* the `cimfs` snapshotter but **cannot pull/unpack** into it, because dockerd's managed containerd is configured with the default diff-service differ order and there's no way to change it. containerd itself fully supports CimFS today — the gap is entirely in how dockerd generates (and locks down) the managed containerd config.

### Environment
- Windows 11 25H2 (build 10.0.26200), Docker Engine 29, dockerd-managed containerd **v2.3.1**.
- Image store enabled: `daemon.json` → `"default-runtime": "io.containerd.runhcs.v1"`, `"features": { "containerd-snapshotter": true }`.

### What works: containerd + CimFS end-to-end (via `ctr`)
containerd ships a complete `cimfs` snapshotter (`plugins/snapshots/windows/cimfs.go`) **and** a complete `cimfs` differ (`plugins/diff/windows/cimfs.go`, `ImportCimLayerFromTar`). Both load on 26200 (`IsCimFSSupported()` = true). With a standalone containerd whose config wires cimfs into the two relevant places:

```toml
[plugins."io.containerd.service.v1.diff-service"]
  default = ["cimfs", "windows", "windows-lcow"]

[[plugins."io.containerd.transfer.v1.local".unpack_config]]
  platform = "windows/amd64"
  snapshotter = "windows"
  differ = "windows"
[[plugins."io.containerd.transfer.v1.local".unpack_config]]
  platform = "windows/amd64"
  snapshotter = "cimfs"
  differ = "cimfs"
```

…a full pull → unpack → run works:

```
ctr --address <pipe> images pull --snapshotter cimfs mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2025   # unpacks via the cimfs differ
ctr --address <pipe> run --rm --snapshotter cimfs mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2025 t cmd /c "echo ok"   # -> ok
```

The runhcs shim mounts the CimFS rootfs (`rootfs type=CimFS`, `mountProcessIsolatedCimLayersCommon`) and the container runs. So the snapshotter, differ, and runtime are all functional on GA Windows.

### What fails: the same thing under dockerd
`DOCKER_DRIVER=cimfs` (the Windows way to pick a non-default snapshotter — `daemon/image_store_choice.go`) makes `docker info` report `Driver=cimfs` and dockerd starts, but any pull fails at unpack:

```
failed to extract layer ... to cimfs ...: Unimplemented: mount layer type must be lcow-layer: not implemented
```

### Root cause
dockerd's own pull-unpack goes through the containerd **diff service**, whose Windows default order is hardcoded to `["windows", "windows-lcow"]` (`plugins/services/diff/service_windows.go`). The `cimfs` differ is registered but never in that order, so a `CimFSMountType` mount is rejected by `windows` (wants `windows-layer`) then `windows-lcow` (wants `lcow-layer`) — that last rejection is the error above.

The order is only fixable via containerd config, but:
- dockerd generates the managed `containerd.toml` purely from an internal struct and applies only a few `DaemonOpt`s (log level/format, CRI-disable, local-binary detection — `daemon/internal/libcontainerd/supervisor/remote_daemon*.go`); **none touch the diff-service or transfer plugins**.
- There is **no daemon.json field** to inject containerd plugin config.
- The file is rewritten (`O_TRUNC`) on every daemon start, so hand-edits don't survive.

So today there is no supported way to make dockerd use CimFS, even though every underlying piece works.

### Why it matters
CimFS mounts read-only `.cim` layer files instead of expanding each layer on disk — a meaningful storage/space win for WCOW images (relevant to the long-standing "Windows images use far more disk than their actual size" complaint) and potentially faster layer setup.

### Proposed fix (options)
1. **Auto-wire** — when the selected image-store snapshotter is a CIM snapshotter, add the matching differ (`cimfs`, and `blockcim` when available) to the managed containerd's diff-service order (and transfer `unpack_config`).
2. **Passthrough** — let operators supply extra containerd plugin config that dockerd merges into the managed `containerd.toml` (would also unblock other snapshotters).

### Forward note: blockcim is already implemented in containerd
The same containerd file registers a `blockcim` snapshotter/differ, and it's complete — it just skip-loads on current GA Windows because hcsshim gates it to build ≥ 27766 (`IsBlockCimSupported()`, an explicit placeholder pending an official build; verified/merged CIM ≥ 27800). So once Windows ships block-CIM support, **no new containerd work is needed** — only the *same* dockerd wiring this issue asks for. Designing the fix to cover both `cimfs` and `blockcim` now avoids a second round later.

### Upstream context
Filed a companion issue on containerd proposing it default-wire these snapshotters when the OS supports them (so this stops being per-downstream boilerplate): containerd/containerd#13782.

title: Flaky test: TestDockerCLIEventSuite/TestEventsContainerRestart (timing race on Windows container restart event)
author: GordonTheTurtle
body:
## Description

`TestEventsContainerRestart` tests that after restarting a container, Docker emits the expected sequence of events (`die`, `start`) in the event stream. On Windows, the test polls for the condition `"false false"` (both die and start events received) but times out after 1m30s, having only reached `"false true"`.

## First observed (in this scan)

2026-07-10, run [29107585158](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29107585158/job/86414801797) (`29.x_backport_bump_rootlesskit_bin`, `run (graphdriver) / integration-test (graphdriver, containerd, DockerCLICommitSuite|...)` job).

## Failure output

```
docker_cli_events_test.go:657: assertion failed: error is not nil:
condition ""false true" == "false false"" not true in time (1m30s)
--- FAIL: TestDockerCLIEventSuite/TestEventsContainerRestart (94.31s)
--- FAIL: TestDockerCLIEventSuite (272.84s)
```

The `"false true"` value means the `die` event was received but the subsequent `start` event was not received within the timeout window. This points to the container restart taking longer than expected on the Windows runner, or an event-ordering race in the Windows containerd runtime.

## Flakiness evidence

The same test **passed** (10.11s) on the equivalent `bump_rootlesskit_bin` branch run in the same 72-hour window:

| Run | Branch | Job | Result |
|-----|--------|-----|--------|
| [29107585158](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29107585158/job/86414801797) | `29.x_backport_bump_rootlesskit_bin` | graphdriver/containerd/DockerCLICommitSuite\|...\|DockerCLIEventSuite | ❌ FAIL (94s) |
| [29107424128](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29107424128/job/86414102124) | `bump_rootlesskit_bin` | graphdriver/containerd/DockerCLICommitSuite\|...\|DockerCLIEventSuite | ✅ PASS (10s) |

## Root cause hypothesis

Timing race: the Windows containerd runtime occasionally delays the `start` event emission after a container restart. The 1m30s timeout in the test is usually sufficient, but under load or on slow runners the event is not delivered in time.

## Related

- #37306 — flaky tests epic
- The test is in `integration/cli/docker_cli_events_test.go` (Windows-compatible path)

_Flagged by automated CI flakiness scan — 2026-07-11._

title: Flaky test: docker-py SwarmTest::test_init_swarm_with_ca_config (swarm left encrypted/locked between tests)
author: GordonTheTurtle
body:
## Description

The docker-py integration test `SwarmTest::test_init_swarm_with_ca_config` fails intermittently when the swarm daemon is left in an **encrypted/locked** state by a preceding test, causing the subsequent `POST /swarm/init` call to return HTTP 503.

## First observed

2026-07-09, run [29032400304](https://github.com/moby/moby/actions/runs/29032400304/job/86168828742) (`bump_bolt` branch, `test (amd64, graphdriver) / docker-py` job).

## Failure output

```
___________________ SwarmTest.test_init_swarm_with_ca_config ___________________

    raise HTTPError(http_error_msg, response=self)
E   requests.exceptions.HTTPError: 503 Server Error: Service Unavailable
    for url: http+docker://localhost/v1.55/swarm/init
E   docker.errors.APIError: 503 Server Error for http+docker://localhost/v1.55/swarm/init:
    Service Unavailable ("Swarm is encrypted and needs to be unlocked
    before it can be used. Please use \"docker swarm unlock\" to unlock it.")
    tests/integration/api_swarm_test.py:91: in test_init_swarm_with_ca_config
```

## Root cause

A prior test in the docker-py suite (likely one of the swarm lock/unlock tests) leaves the daemon's swarm in an encrypted, locked state. When `test_init_swarm_with_ca_config` runs next and calls `swarm/init`, the daemon refuses with 503 because it first needs to be unlocked.

This is a **test isolation / teardown** problem — the test suite does not reliably unlock or leave the swarm in a clean state before the next test runs.

## Related

- #34051 — `TestSwarmLockUnlockCluster` (Go integration-cli) shares the same root family: swarm lock state leaking between tests
- #37306 — flaky tests epic

## Reproduction

Run the full docker-py integration suite (`make test-docker-py`) multiple times; the failure is environment- and ordering-sensitive.

_Flagged by automated CI flakiness scan._

title: There is no way to configure container disk size on Windows when containerd storage is used a.k.a. 20GB limit strikes back
author: slonopotamus

### Description

For graphdriver, we have `{ "storage-opts": ["size=100500GB"] }` in `daemon.json`. For containerd snapshotter there is nothing, so containers have fixed 20GB limit without any way to configure.

title: Docker 29 dind-rootless fails to start containers on Linux < 5.11 kernel due to containerd snapshotter regression
author: okhowang
body:
### Description

Docker 29 introduces a breaking change for dind-rootless on Linux < 5.11 kernels. The default enablement of containerd image store (containerd snapshotter) causes container startup failures with "operation not permitted" errors when mounting overlay filesystems in rootless environments.

### Reproduce

1. Run dind-rootless on a Linux < 5.11 kernel:
```bash
docker run --name dind -e DOCKER_TLS_CERTDIR='' -it --rm --privileged docker:29.6.1-dind-rootless dockerd
```

2. Attempt to start a container:
```bash
docker exec -it -eDOCKER_HOST=unix:///run/user/1000/docker.sock dind docker run -it --rm alpine
``

### Expected behavior

Containers should start successfully, falling back to fuse-overlayfs when kernel overlay support is unavailable (as Docker 28 did).

### docker version

### Additional Info

### Docker 28 Behavior (Working)
- Used graphdriver path (overlay2) by default
- Automatically fell back to fuse-overlayfs when kernel overlay support was unavailable
- Implemented in `overlayutils.SupportsOverlay()` with fallback logic

### Docker 29 Behavior (Broken)
- Defaults to containerd image store (containerd snapshotter)
- Containerd's overlayfs snapshotter directly calls `mount("overlay", ...)` without fallback logic
- Returns `EPERM` on kernels < 5.11 that lack unprivileged overlayfs support

The issue is that **containerd's overlayfs snapshotter lacks the automatic fallback mechanism** that Docker's graphdriver path had. While the underlying kernel limitation (no unprivileged overlayfs in kernel < 5.11) exists in both versions, Docker 28 handled it gracefully while Docker 29 does not.

title: Improve the Build API for v2/Buildkit-Backend
author: AB-xdev
body:
### Description

Context: I'm currently basically trying to implement `docker build` using Testcontainers which itself uses the Docker API. This is quite limited and this constantly causes me to e.g. build workarounds or sit around and wait because it caching is not working properly.

Currently the following API is exposed by Docker Engine to build an image: https://docs.docker.com/reference/api/engine/version/v1.55/#tag/Image/operation/ImageBuild

This API has some serious shortcomings:
* By default it uses `version=1` - the "first generation classic (deprecated) builder in the Docker daemon"
  * This builder lacks features that modern Dockerfiles may contain. e.g `COPY --parents`
  * Doing caching with it is kind of impossible/extremely complex for [multi-stage Dockerfiles](https://andrewlock.net/caching-docker-layers-on-serverless-build-hosts-with-multi-stage-builds---target,-and---cache-from/)

As time progresses this makes the API more and more unfit for use.

However you can also call the API with `version=2` - this uses the Buildkit backend. However this has other problems:
* As the API still only has the parameters of `version=1` it's impossible to use new Buildkit features like `cache-from`, `cache-to`
* The response is no longer streamed as it was in v1 - you get all output (including logs) at the end and have no idea what is going on in the meantime
* The logs are returned protobuf-encoded - a normal REST client can't properly decode them, without knowing what they are
* This endpoint [seems to be deprecated](https://github.com/docker-java/docker-java/pull/2362#issuecomment-2546324924) in the first place

Then there is the Buildkit gRPC API... I did some research and as far as I know the only 3rd party project that ever managed to successfully implemented it seems to be https://github.com/fussybeaver/bollard.
* I found no other working implementations but [some (not working) experiments](https://github.com/cowwoc/anchor4j/tree/buildkit-grpc).
* The [documentation of the API is effectively non-existant](https://github.com/moby/buildkit/issues/5704). For example:
  * `SolveRequest#FrontendInputs#FrontendInputs` is of type `map<string, pb.Definition>` and has no indications what it should contain.
  * `SolveRequest#FrontendInputs` seems to require a `session` yet it's never explained where you can get one or how it needs to be handled (start, end)
  * Until recently you also had to know that you have to call `/grpc` to make grpc work

I had a look at it and it's way too complex to implement when compared to something like the existing buildImage-endpoint and requires at least 10-20 times the effort because you have to port the entire gRPC API.

I would therefore like to have a `buildImageV2` endpoint that improves on the existing endpoint by adding new options (from `docker build`).
This way most of the logic is kept on the engine's side and it's easier for users to implement the API.

Alternatively - if it's "not that hard" to implement the gRPC API:
Could you provide a simple minimal standalone example how to build an image using the API? Preferably in a higher level language that most devs know like Java or JavaScript.

title: Plugin.ScopedPath should reject traversal outside PropagatedMount root
author: SylvanJu
body:
### Description

`Plugin.ScopedPath` appears to validate plugin-returned mountpoints with `strings.HasPrefix`, but then builds the host-side path with `filepath.Join`.

For a v2 volume plugin configured with `PropagatedMount=/propagated`, a plugin can return a mountpoint such as:

```text
/propagated/../../../volumes/victimvol/_data
```

This passes the prefix check, but `filepath.Join` normalizes the `..` components, so the final host-side path can escape the intended `PropagatedMount` root.

The relevant code appears to be:

```go
func (p *Plugin) ScopedPath(s string) string {
        if p.PluginObj.Config.PropagatedMount != "" && strings.HasPrefix(s, p.PluginObj.Config.PropagatedMount) {
                return filepath.Join(filepath.Dir(p.Rootfs), "propagated-mount", strings.TrimPrefix(s, p.PluginObj.Config.PropagatedMount))
        }
        return filepath.Join(p.Rootfs, s)
}
```

### Reproduce

1. Create a Docker volume used as the out-of-scope target:

```bash
sudo docker volume create victimvol
echo "VICTIM_VOLUME_SECRET" | sudo tee /var/lib/docker/volumes/victimvol/_data/secret.txt
```

2. Create and enable a v2 volume plugin with:

```text
PropagatedMount=/propagated
```

3. Make the plugin return this value from both `VolumeDriver.Mount` and `VolumeDriver.Path`:

```text
/propagated/../../../volumes/victimvol/_data
```

4. Create a volume using that plugin:

```bash
sudo docker volume create -d test/plugin testvol
```

5. Mount the plugin-backed volume into a regular container:

```bash
sudo docker run --rm -v testvol:/mnt/test busybox cat /mnt/test/secret.txt
```

Observed output:

```text
VICTIM_VOLUME_SECRET
```


### Expected behavior

Docker should reject plugin-returned mountpoints whose normalized/resolved final host-side path escapes the plugin's declared `PropagatedMount` root.

A string prefix check should not be used as the only containment check before path normalization.

title: Overlay VXLAN packets have source address chosen by kernel but the Swarm data-path address is the only correct answer
author: andersonlemesc
body:
### Description

On a 3-node Swarm cluster running **Ubuntu 26.04 LTS (kernel 7.0.0-generic)**, **no overlay traffic crosses between nodes**. Same-node container-to-container works perfectly; any cross-node communication times out.

The VXLAN encapsulated packet **arrives at the destination host's NIC** (visible with `tcpdump`), but the per-network `vxlan0` device inside the overlay netns **receives nothing** — `ip -s link show vxlan0` reports **RX packets = 0** on every overlay network (`ingress`, user overlays, freshly created overlays), on every node. Decapsulation into the netns never happens.

Crucially, **`dockerd` logs no error** — it only logs `initialized VXLAN UDP port to 4789`, i.e. it believes the overlay was set up correctly. The failure is silent in the data path.

This affects **both Swarm services and standalone `--attachable` containers** (this is what distinguishes it from #50282, where services reportedly still work).

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS (resolute)
- Kernel: `7.0.0-14-generic` / `7.0.0-27-generic`
- Docker: `docker.io 29.1.3` (Ubuntu package). **Also reproduced with `docker-ce 28.5.2`** (installed from the official noble repo) — same failure, so it is not version-specific to 29.x.
- containerd: 2.2.2 (Ubuntu) / containerd.io 2.2.5 (with docker-ce)
- 3 managers, VXLAN data-path port 4789
- No firewall (ufw inactive, no iptables/nft DROP for 4789), no overlay encryption (`ip xfrm` empty)

### What was ruled out (tested)

- **Firewall / IPSec / MTU / NIC offload** — none (VXLAN offload already `off [fixed]`, MTU fine).
- **Underlay** — ICMP between all nodes is 0% loss; control plane (2377, 7946 TCP+UDP) healthy; `docker network inspect <net> .Peers` consistent across nodes.
- **Data-path address** — reproduced with data-path on a routed loopback /32 **and** on a directly-connected LAN address; both fail.
- **Docker version** — 29.1.3 and 28.5.2 both fail identically.
- **Kernel VXLAN itself is fine** — a hand-made VXLAN tunnel (see below) works cross-node, including inside a netns and even on `dstport 4789`. Only Docker's overlay fails.

### Key evidence: vanilla VXLAN works, Docker overlay does not

Manual VXLAN between two nodes (no Docker) — **works, RX > 0**:

```bash
# node A
sudo ip link add vxt type vxlan id 9990 local <A-ip> remote <B-ip> dstport 4789
sudo ip addr add 192.168.200.1/24 dev vxt; sudo ip link set vxt up
# node B (mirror), then:
ping 192.168.200.1          # 0% loss
ip -s link show vxt         # RX packets > 0
```

The same works when the vxlan device is created and then moved into a separate netns (mimicking Docker), and on `dstport 4789`. So the kernel's VXLAN receive path is functional; Docker's overlay `vxlan0` (in the network netns) receives nothing.

There is a stray `0.0.0.0:4789 UNCONN` UDP socket in the host netns with no associated vxlan device (`ip -d link show type vxlan` in the host netns is empty), which appears to consume the incoming 4789 datagrams instead of them reaching the per-network `vxlan0` in its netns.

### Minimal reproduction (self-contained — any Ubuntu 26.04 + Docker + 2 nodes)

```bash
# on manager
docker network create -d overlay repro
docker service create --name repro --network repro --replicas 2 \
  --constraint 'node.hostname!=<manager-or-any-single-node>' \
  nicolaka/netshoot sleep 3600
# find the two task IPs (they land on two different nodes) and ping across:
#   docker exec <taskA> ping <taskB-overlay-ip>   -> 100% packet loss

# confirm decap never happens on the receiving node:
NS=$(docker network inspect repro -f '{{.Id}}' | cut -c1-10)
sudo nsenter --net=/run/docker/netns/1-$NS ip -s link show vxlan0   # RX packets = 0

# meanwhile the encapsulated packet IS arriving at the host:
sudo tcpdump -nni any udp port 4789   # shows inbound VXLAN with vni + inner ICMP/TCP
```

### Related reports we reviewed — none is an exact match

We searched existing issues before filing. The following are related but **none matches our case**:

- **#52289 / #52265 / #52661** — stale ARP/FDB entries causing *intermittent* 502/504 that a redeploy clears (29.3.0 regression, fixed in 29.4.1). Ours is **total and permanent** (RX=0), and reproduces on 28.5.2 too — so it is not that regression.
- **#49555** — `error creating vxlan interface: file exists` (interface creation fails). Ours **creates** the interface fine; it just never receives.
- **#50282** — closest *symptom* (packets reach the host but not the container netns), but there **services work and only standalone `--attachable` fails**. In our case **services also fail** and `vxlan0` RX is 0, i.e. decapsulation itself never happens.
- **#29979** — closest *mechanism* (unidirectional overlay, `Could not open netlink handle during vni population ... failed to set into network namespace ... invalid argument`), but that is very old (Docker 1.13, NAT traversal) and the netlink error is **logged**. In our case **dockerd logs no error at all** — setup silently reports success.

So this appears to be a distinct, silent Docker-overlay × kernel-7.0 / Ubuntu-26.04 incompatibility not covered by the above.

### Ask

Is this a known incompatibility between the overlay driver's VXLAN/netns setup and newer kernels (7.0) / Ubuntu 26.04? Any diagnostic that would confirm whether the per-network `vxlan0` socket is being shadowed by the stray host-netns 4789 socket? Happy to run additional tests — the cluster stays on this stack for now.

### Exact environment (from an affected node)

```
$ uname -a
Linux cluster1 7.0.0-14-generic #14-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Mon Apr 13 11:09:53 UTC 2026 x86_64 GNU/Linux

$ . /etc/os-release; echo "$PRETTY_NAME / $VERSION_CODENAME"
Ubuntu 26.04 LTS / resolute

Server Version:  29.1.3   (also reproduced with docker-ce 28.5.2)
API:             1.52
containerd:      2.2.2     (containerd.io 2.2.5 when on docker-ce)
runc:            1.4.0
Cgroup:          systemd / v2
Swarm:           active
Default Pool:    10.0.0.0/8
```

vxlan0 inside the overlay netns (created without error, yet RX=0):

```
vxlan0@if...: mtu 1450 ... master br0 state UNKNOWN
    vxlan id 4096 srcport 0 0 dstport 4789 ttl auto ageing 300 proxy l2miss l3miss
    ... learning on flood on ...
```

Stray host-netns socket that appears to swallow inbound 4789:

```
$ sudo ss -ulnp | grep 4789
UNCONN 0  0  0.0.0.0:4789  0.0.0.0:*        # no process, no vxlan dev in host netns

$ sudo ip -d link show type vxlan           # (in host netns) -> empty
```

dockerd only logs (no error):

```
level=info msg="initialized VXLAN UDP port to 4789 " module=node
```

### Reproduction environment note

This does **not** require special hardware or network topology. It was originally seen on a fiber point-to-point mesh with a routed loopback data-path, but it reproduces identically with the data-path on a plain directly-connected LAN address. Two Ubuntu 26.04 (kernel 7.0) VMs joined into a Swarm with a single overlay are sufficient to reproduce.

author: OYTAdmin
body:
### Description

I noticed that if I delete all of the log streams in my ECS service's log group, then ECS stops logging until I kill/restart the service. This is an unexpected behavior compared to other AWS services that will create a new log stream and continue logging when log streams are deleted.

I believe the solution is to catch the error when it happens, and if the error is ResourceNotFoundException on the log stream, then recreate the stream (or create a new one) and submit the batch again.

I think the relevant code is at https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/258ee34b765719cdbfa4c6315fc554c7fbc67e37/daemon/logger/awslogs/cloudwatchlogs.go#L709

This would be a nice quality of life improvement for those of us who are testing code running in ECS.

title: client/progress: time-remaining display is dead code because progress.Progress has no Start field
author: htoyoda18
body:
## Description

The progress reporting system has a time-remaining display feature that is fully implemented on the **rendering side** but is effectively dead code because the `Start` field is never populated on the **source side**.

### Current state

`jsonstream.Progress` already has a `Start int64` field (Unix timestamp), and both renderers correctly use it to calculate and display estimated time remaining:

- `client/pkg/streamformatter/streamformatter.go` (raw progress formatter)
- `client/pkg/jsonmessage/jsonmessage.go` (TUI progress formatter)

```go
// This logic exists in both renderers but is never reached
if p.Current > 0 && p.Start > 0 && percentage < 50 {
    fromStart := time.Since(time.Unix(p.Start, 0))
    perEntry := fromStart / time.Duration(p.Current)
    left := time.Duration(p.Total-p.Current) * perEntry
    timeLeftBox = " " + left.Round(time.Second).String()
}
```

However, the internal `progress.Progress` struct (used by both client and daemon) has **no `Start` field**, and the conversion layer that maps `progress.Progress` → `jsonstream.Progress` never populates `Start`. As a result, `p.Start` is always `0`, the condition `p.Start > 0` is always `false`, and the time-remaining display is never shown.

There is also a FIXME comment noting this:

```go
// client/pkg/streamformatter/streamformatter.go:146
// FIXME(thaJeztah): p.Start is never set, because progress.Progress doesn't have this field
```

### Proposed fix

Complete the feature by connecting the `Start` field end-to-end:

1. **Add `Start int64` to `progress.Progress`** — both `client/pkg/progress/progress.go` and `daemon/internal/progress/progress.go`
2. **Pass `Start` through the conversion layer** — in `WriteProgress` of both `client/pkg/streamformatter/streamformatter.go` and `daemon/internal/streamformatter/streamformatter.go`
3. **Populate `Start` at the source**:
   - `client/pkg/progress/progressreader.go` — record `time.Now()` in `NewProgressReader`, emit as `Start` in `updateProgress`
   - `daemon/internal/progress/progressreader.go` — same as above
   - `daemon/containerd/progress.go` — a `start time.Time` parameter is already passed to `UpdateProgress` but unused; use it for Downloading and Extracting progress
   - `daemon/pkg/plugin/fetch_linux.go` — capture `time.Now()` at the start of the download goroutine
   - `daemon/pkg/plugin/backend_linux.go` — `contentStatus.StartedAt` already exists; use it

### Questions for discussion

- Is this the right approach, or should the time-remaining feature be removed instead?
- For the `daemon/containerd/progress.go` push progress (Pushing status), there is currently no per-layer start time tracking. Should we add a `pushStarted map[string]time.Time` to the `pushProgress` struct similar to the existing `unpackStart` map used for Extracting?
- Should we add a `Start` field to `progress.Progress` and have callers set it explicitly, or should `ProgressReader` derive it automatically from `time.Now()` at construction time (and leave direct `WriteProgress` callers without time-remaining support)?

### Test coverage

Existing tests already validate that the rendering logic produces correct output when `Start` is set (see `client/pkg/jsonmessage/jsonmessage_test.go`). New tests would be needed to verify:
- `ProgressReader` populates `Start` correctly
- The conversion layer passes `Start` through

title: docker pull fails at 99% complete, then deletes everything
author: jpeg729
body:
### Description

I have docker running on my dgx spark.
I tried pulling an image, it failed after downloading most of the layers, so I tried pulling again, and it started over.
I have confirmed that I had >200Gb free disk space in addition to the 30Gb download.
I have confirmed that the disk space was released after each failure.
The logs confirm that docker explicitly cleaned up the downloaded layers.
I was not given the option of keeping the partial state and trying again.
After a couple of tries I set `{ "max-concurrent-downloads": 1 }` in `/etc/docker/daemon.json`. This changed the downloading behaviour but did not solve the problem.

Conclusion: 4 or 5 tries. ~2h each time. Many, many GB of download bandwidth consumed for nothing.
Suffice to say this is VERY ANNOYING.

In the end chatgpt helped me use `ctr image pull` then export and import to docker, but I shouldn't need to faff around like that.

### Reproduce

1. I ran docker pull ghcr.io/aeon-7/aeon-vllm-ultimate:latest
2. It failed with "unexpected EOF" when it has nearly completed.
3. I ran docker pull .. again
4. It started downloading everything again.


### Expected behavior

It should not delete everything, it should acknowledge the failure, and tell me to either retry or run docker image prune or something.
